question: A long-running daemon on your production system accumulates hundreds of zombie processes. What causes zombie processes, how would you identify them, and what steps would you take to eliminate them without restarting the parent daemon?
answer: A zombie is a terminated process whose exit status was not read by its parent via wait or waitpid. Identify zombies using ps aux with awk filtering for Z in the STAT column or check the State field in the /proc filesystem. Zombies cannot be killed with SIGKILL since they are already dead. Attach to the parent with gdb and call waitpid(-1, 0, WNOHANG) to reap them, or send SIGCHLD if the parent has a handler. The remaining options are restarting the parent or reparenting to init which reaps orphans.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Parent forks child] --> B[Child runs and exits]
  B --> C{Parent calls waitpid?}
  C -->|Yes| D[Process table entry freed]
  C -->|No| E[Child becomes ZOMBIE state Z]
  E --> F[Zombie cannot be killed with SIGKILL]
  F --> G{Resolution path}
  G -->|Send SIGCHLD| H[Parent handler reaps zombie]
  G -->|Attach gdb + waitpid call| H
  G -->|Kill parent| I[Orphan adopted by init]
  G -->|Restart parent| J[Fixed parent reaps orphans]
  I --> K[Init calls waitpid, reaps zombie]
  H --> D
  J --> D
  K --> D
